question:
\[
T_0 = 2, T_1 = 3, T_2 = 6,
\]
and for $n \geq 3$,
\[
T_n = (n+4)T_{n-1} - 4n T_{n-2} + (4n-8) T_{n-3}.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
2, 3, 6, 14, 40, 152, 784, 5168, 40576.
\]
Find, with proof, a formula for $T_n$ of the form $T_n = A_n + B_n$,
where $\{A_n\}$ and $\{B_n\}$ are well-known sequences.
solution:
Solution. The formula \( T_{n}=n!+2^{n} \) can be verified by induction.
Alternatively, set \( t_{n}=n!+2^{n} \). Clearly \( t_{0}=2=T_{0}, t_{1}=3=T_{1} \) and \( t_{2}=6=T_{2} \). Also,
\[
t_{n}-n t_{n-1}=2^{n}-n 2^{n-1}
\]

Now \( 2^{n} \) and \( n 2^{n-1} \) are both solutions of the linear recursion
\[
f_{n}-4 f_{n-1}+4 f_{n-2}=0
\]
this follows from direct substitution. Since \( t_{n}-n t_{n-1} \) is a linear combination of solutions to (1), it must also be a solution. Hence
\[
\left(t_{n}-n t_{n-1}\right)-4\left(t_{n-1}-(n-1) t_{n-2}\right)+4\left(t_{n-2}-(n-2) t_{n-3}\right)=0
\]
or equivalently,
\[
t_{n}=(n+4) t_{n-1}-4 n t_{n-2}+(4 n-8) t_{n-3}
\]

Thus \( t_{n}=T_{n} \), because they are identical for \( n=0,1,2 \) and satisfy the same third-order recursion (1) for \( n \geq 3 \).

Remark. Let \( \left\{t_{n}^{(1)}\right\}_{n \geq 1},\left\{t_{n}^{(2)}\right\}_{n \geq 1}, \ldots,\left\{t_{n}^{(m)}\right\}_{n \geq 1} \) be sequences, each of one of the following forms:
(i) \( \left\{\alpha^{n}\right\}_{n \geq 1} \) for some \( \alpha \in \mathbb{C} \),
(ii) \( \{P(n)\}_{n \geq 1} \) for some polynomial \( P \in \mathbb{C}[n] \),
(iii) \( \{(a n+b)!\}_{n \geq 1} \) for some integers \( a, b \geq 0 \).

Let \( Q \in \mathbb{C}\left[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}\right] \) be a polynomial, and define \( u_{n}=Q\left(t_{n}^{(1)}, \ldots, t_{n}^{(m)}\right) \) for \( n \geq 1 \). Then one can show that the sequences \( \left\{u_{n}\right\}_{n \geq 1},\left\{u_{n+1}\right\}_{n \geq 1},\left\{u_{n+2}\right\}_{n \geq 1}, \ldots \), thought of as functions of \( n \), lie in a finitely generated \( \mathbb{C}[n] \)-submodule of the \( \mathbb{C}[n] \)-module of all sequences of complex numbers. Therefore \( \left\{u_{n}\right\} \) satisfies a nontrivial linear recursion with polynomial coefficients. The reader may enjoy finding this recursion explicitly for sequences such as \( (n!)^{2}+3^{n} \) or \( 2^{n} n!+F_{n} \) where \( F_{n} \) is the \( n \)th Fibonacci number, defined at the end of 1988A5. Problem 1984B1 [PutnamII, p. 44] is a variation on this theme:

Let \( n \) be a positive integer, and define
\[
f(n)=1!+2!+\cdots+n!
\]

Find polynomials \( P(x) \) and \( Q(x) \) such that
\[
f(n+2)=P(n) f(n+1)+Q(n) f(n)
\]
for all \( n \geq 1 \).

descriptive_long.question:
\[
seqtzero = 2, seqtone = 3, seqttwo = 6,
\]
and for $indexvar \geq 3$,
\[
seqtelem = (indexvar+4)seqtprev - 4 indexvar seqtprevtwo + (4 indexvar-8) seqtprevthr.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
2, 3, 6, 14, 40, 152, 784, 5168, 40576.
\]
Find, with proof, a formula for $seqtelem$ of the form $seqtelem = seqaelem + seqbelem$,
where $\{seqaelem\}$ and $\{seqbelem\}$ are well-known sequences.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. The formula \( seqtelem=indexvar!+2^{indexvar} \) can be verified by induction.
Alternatively, set \( auxtelem=indexvar!+2^{indexvar} \). Clearly \( auxtzero=2=seqtzero, auxtone=3=seqtone \) and \( auxttwo=6=seqttwo \). Also,
\[
auxtelem-indexvar auxtprev=2^{indexvar}-indexvar 2^{indexvar-1}
\]

Now \( 2^{indexvar} \) and \( indexvar 2^{indexvar-1} \) are both solutions of the linear recursion
\[
seqfelem-4 seqfprev+4 seqfprevtwo=0
\]
this follows from direct substitution. Since \( auxtelem-indexvar auxtprev \) is a linear combination of solutions to (1), it must also be a solution. Hence
\[
\left(auxtelem-indexvar auxtprev\right)-4\left(auxtprev-(indexvar-1) auxtprevtwo\right)+4\left(auxtprevtwo-(indexvar-2) auxtprevthr\right)=0
\]
or equivalently,
\[
auxtelem=(indexvar+4) auxtprev-4 indexvar auxtprevtwo+(4 indexvar-8) auxtprevthr
\]

Thus \( auxtelem=seqtelem \), because they are identical for \( indexvar=0,1,2 \) and satisfy the same third-order recursion (1) for \( indexvar \geq 3 \).

Remark. Let \( \{auxtelem^{(1)}\}_{indexvar \geq 1},\{auxtelem^{(2)}\}_{indexvar \geq 1}, \ldots,\{auxtelem^{(m)}\}_{indexvar \geq 1} \) be sequences, each of one of the following forms:
(i) \( \{alphaparam^{indexvar}\}_{indexvar \geq 1} \) for some \( alphaparam \in \mathbb{C} \),
(ii) \( \{polynomp(indexvar)\}_{indexvar \geq 1} \) for some polynomial \( polynomp \in \mathbb{C}[indexvar] \),
(iii) \( \{(intparama indexvar+intparamb)!\}_{indexvar \geq 1} \) for some integers \( intparama, intparamb \geq 0 \).

Let \( polynomyq \in \mathbb{C}[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}] \) be a polynomial, and define \( sequelem=polynomyq(auxtelem^{(1)}, \ldots, auxtelem^{(m)}) \) for \( indexvar \geq 1 \). Then one can show that the sequences \( \{sequelem\}_{indexvar \geq 1},\{sequnext\}_{indexvar \geq 1},\{sequplus2\}_{indexvar \geq 1}, \ldots \), thought of as functions of \( indexvar \), lie in a finitely generated \( \mathbb{C}[indexvar] \)-submodule of the \( \mathbb{C}[indexvar] \)-module of all sequences of complex numbers. Therefore \( \{sequelem\} \) satisfies a nontrivial linear recursion with polynomial coefficients. The reader may enjoy finding this recursion explicitly for sequences such as \( (indexvar!)^{2}+3^{indexvar} \) or \( 2^{indexvar} indexvar!+fibnelem \) where \( fibnelem \) is the \( indexvar \)th Fibonacci number, defined at the end of 1988A5. Problem 1984B1 [PutnamII, p. 44] is a variation on this theme:

Let \( indexvar \) be a positive integer, and define
\[
f(indexvar)=1!+2!+\cdots+indexvar!
\]

Find polynomials \( polynomp(x) \) and \( polynomyq(x) \) such that
\[
f(indexvar+2)=polynomp(indexvar) f(indexvar+1)+polynomyq(indexvar) f(indexvar)
\]
for all \( indexvar \geq 1 \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
\[
crossroad = 2, hairbrush = 3, peppermint = 6,
\]
and for $telescope \geq 3$,
\[
windmill = (telescope+4)snowball - 4telescope lighthouse + (4telescope-8) saxophone.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
2, 3, 6, 14, 40, 152, 784, 5168, 40576.
\]
Find, with proof, a formula for $windmill$ of the form $windmill = pineapple + typewriter$,
where $\{pineapple\}$ and $\{typewriter\}$ are well-known sequences.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. The formula \( windmill = telescope! + 2^{telescope} \) can be verified by induction.
Alternatively, set \( porcupine = telescope! + 2^{telescope} \). Clearly \( cornfield = 2 = crossroad,\, waterfall = 3 = hairbrush \) and \( skyladder = 6 = peppermint \). Also,
\[
porcupine - telescope\, playhouse = 2^{telescope} - telescope\, 2^{telescope-1}
\]

Now \( 2^{telescope} \) and \( telescope\, 2^{telescope-1} \) are both solutions of the linear recursion
\[
sandboxer - 4\, moonlight + 4\, rainstorm = 0
\]
this follows from direct substitution. Since \( porcupine - telescope\, playhouse \) is a linear combination of solutions to (1), it must also be a solution. Hence
\[
\left(porcupine - telescope\, playhouse\right) - 4\left(playhouse - (telescope-1)\, butterfly\right) + 4\left(butterfly - (telescope-2)\, drumstick\right)=0
\]
or equivalently,
\[
porcupine = (telescope+4)\, playhouse - 4\, telescope\, butterfly + (4\, telescope - 8)\, drumstick
\]

Thus \( porcupine = windmill \), because they are identical for \( telescope = 0,1,2 \) and satisfy the same third-order recursion (1) for \( telescope \geq 3 \).

Remark. Let \( \{porcupine^{(1)}\}_{telescope \geq 1},\{porcupine^{(2)}\}_{telescope \geq 1}, \ldots,\{porcupine^{(m)}\}_{telescope \geq 1} \) be sequences, each of one of the following forms:
(i) \( \{roadrunner^{telescope}\}_{telescope \geq 1} \) for some \( roadrunner \in \mathbb{C} \),
(ii) \( \{dragonfly(telescope)\}_{telescope \geq 1} \) for some polynomial \( dragonfly \in \mathbb{C}[telescope] \),
(iii) \( \{(honeycomb\, telescope + newspaper)!\}_{telescope \geq 1} \) for some integers \( honeycomb, newspaper \geq 0 \).

Let \( cheeseboard \in \mathbb{C}[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}] \) be a polynomial, and define \( stargazer = cheeseboard(porcupine^{(1)}, \ldots, porcupine^{(m)}) \) for \( telescope \geq 1 \). Then one can show that the sequences \( \{stargazer\}_{telescope \geq 1},\{bookstore\}_{telescope \geq 1},\{earthling\}_{telescope \geq 1}, \ldots \), thought of as functions of \( telescope \), lie in a finitely generated \( \mathbb{C}[telescope] \)-submodule of the \( \mathbb{C}[telescope] \)-module of all sequences of complex numbers. Therefore \( \{stargazer\} \) satisfies a nontrivial linear recursion with polynomial coefficients. The reader may enjoy finding this recursion explicitly for sequences such as \( (telescope!)^{2} + 3^{telescope} \) or \( 2^{telescope}\, telescope! + framework \) where \( framework \) is the \( telescope \)th Fibonacci number, defined at the end of 1988A5. Problem 1984B1 [PutnamII, p.~44] is a variation on this theme:

Let \( telescope \) be a positive integer, and define
\[
f(telescope) = 1! + 2! + \cdots + telescope!
\]

Find polynomials \( dragonfly(x) \) and \( cheeseboard(x) \) such that
\[
f(telescope+2) = dragonfly(telescope) f(telescope+1) + cheeseboard(telescope) f(telescope)
\]
for all \( telescope \geq 1 \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
\[
immutablevaluezero = 2, immutablevalueone = 3, immutablevaluetwo = 6,
\]
and for $continuum \geq 3$,
\[
immutablevalue = (continuum+4)immutablevalueprev - 4continuum immutablevaluepreprev + (4continuum-8) immutablevalueprepreprev.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
2, 3, 6, 14, 40, 152, 784, 5168, 40576.
\]
Find, with proof, a formula for $immutablevalue$ of the form $immutablevalue = terminusseq + originseq$,
where $\{terminusseq\}$ and $\{originseq\}$ are well-known sequences.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. The formula \( immutablevalue = continuum!+2^{continuum} \) can be verified by induction.
Alternatively, set \( persistentvalue = continuum!+2^{continuum} \). Clearly \( persistentvaluezero =2=immutablevaluezero, persistentvalueone =3=immutablevalueone \) and \( persistentvaluetwo =6=immutablevaluetwo \). Also,
\[
persistentvalue- continuum persistentvalueprev = 2^{continuum} - continuum 2^{continuum-1}
\]

Now \( 2^{continuum} \) and \( continuum 2^{continuum-1} \) are both solutions of the linear recursion
\[
staticdata-4 staticdataprev+4 staticdatapreprev=0
\]
this follows from direct substitution. Since \( persistentvalue- continuum persistentvalueprev \) is a linear combination of solutions to (1), it must also be a solution. Hence
\[
\left(persistentvalue- continuum persistentvalueprev\right)-4\left(persistentvalueprev-(continuum-1) persistentvaluepreprev\right)+4\left(persistentvaluepreprev-(continuum-2) persistentvalueprepreprev\right)=0
\]
or equivalently,
\[
persistentvalue=(continuum+4) persistentvalueprev-4 continuum persistentvaluepreprev+(4 continuum-8) persistentvalueprepreprev
\]

Thus \( persistentvalue=immutablevalue \), because they are identical for \( continuum=0,1,2 \) and satisfy the same third-order recursion (1) for \( continuum \geq 3 \).

Remark. Let \( \left\{stationaryvalue^{(1)}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1},\left\{stationaryvalue^{(2)}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1}, \ldots,\left\{stationaryvalue^{(m)}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1} \) be sequences, each of one of the following forms:
(i) \( \left\{oppositealpha^{continuum}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1} \) for some \( oppositealpha \in \mathbb{C} \),
(ii) \( \{nonpolynomial(continuum)\}_{continuum \geq 1} \) for some polynomial \( nonpolynomial \in \mathbb{C}[continuum] \),
(iii) \( \{(antistart continuum+nonalpha)!\}_{continuum \geq 1} \) for some integers \( antistart, nonalpha \geq 0 \).

Let \( nonlinear \in \mathbb{C}\left[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}\right] \) be a polynomial, and define \( staticmember = nonlinear\left(stationaryvalue^{(1)}, \ldots, stationaryvalue^{(m)}\right) \) for \( continuum \geq 1 \). Then one can show that the sequences \( \left\{staticmember\right\}_{continuum \geq 1},\left\{staticmember_{continuum+1}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1},\left\{staticmember_{continuum+2}\right\}_{continuum \geq 1}, \ldots \), thought of as functions of \( continuum \), lie in a finitely generated \( \mathbb{C}[continuum] \)-submodule of the \( \mathbb{C}[continuum] \)-module of all sequences of complex numbers. Therefore \( \left\{staticmember\right\} \) satisfies a nontrivial linear recursion with polynomial coefficients. The reader may enjoy finding this recursion explicitly for sequences such as \( (continuum!)^{2}+3^{continuum} \) or \( 2^{continuum} continuum!+nonfibonacciseq \) where \( nonfibonacciseq \) is the \( continuum \)th Fibonacci number, defined at the end of 1988A5. Problem 1984B1 [PutnamII, p. 44] is a variation on this theme:

Let \( continuum \) be a positive integer, and define
\[
f(continuum)=1!+2!+\cdots+continuum!
\]

Find polynomials \( nonpolynomial_1(x) \) and \( nonlinear_1(x) \) such that
\[
f(continuum+2)=nonpolynomial_1(continuum) f(continuum+1)+nonlinear_1(continuum) f(continuum)
\]
for all \( continuum \geq 1 \).

garbled_string.question:
\[
cmtpyrav = 2, ejhrabnf = 3, tmzcxgou = 6,
\]
and for $lkjhqzpw \geq 3$,
\[
vrmxtspo = (lkjhqzpw+4)zcytqubd - 4lkjhqzpw \, obekxgmi + (4lkjhqzpw-8) \, afpnrewq.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
2, 3, 6, 14, 40, 152, 784, 5168, 40576.
\]
Find, with proof, a formula for $vrmxtspo$ of the form $vrmxtspo = huclyzva + qsdvnxmi$,
where $\{huclyzva\}$ and $\{qsdvnxmi\}$ are well-known sequences.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. The formula \( vrmxtspo = lkjhqzpw! + 2^{lkjhqzpw} \) can be verified by induction.  
Alternatively, set \( pgfwceho = lkjhqzpw! + 2^{lkjhqzpw} \). Clearly \( kabnqvhs = 2 = cmtpyrav, \, lfunwxed = 3 = ejhrabnf \) and \( hazrkgom = 6 = tmzcxgou \). Also,
\[
pgfwceho - lkjhqzpw\, gmbzlerq = 2^{lkjhqzpw} - lkjhqzpw \, 2^{lkjhqzpw-1}
\]
Now \( 2^{lkjhqzpw} \) and \( lkjhqzpw \, 2^{lkjhqzpw-1} \) are both solutions of the linear recursion
\[
wqktsdge - 4 \, yvbanhfr + 4 \, dnqsimob = 0
\]
this follows from direct substitution. Since \( pgfwceho - lkjhqzpw\, gmbzlerq \) is a linear combination of solutions to (1), it must also be a solution. Hence
\[
\left(pgfwceho - lkjhqzpw\, gmbzlerq\right) - 4\left(gmbzlerq - (lkjhqzpw-1)\, jvrwhtco\right) + 4\left(jvrwhtco - (lkjhqzpw-2)\, fkymaqux\right) = 0
\]
or equivalently,
\[
pgfwceho = (lkjhqzpw+4) \, gmbzlerq - 4\, lkjhqzpw \, jvrwhtco + (4\, lkjhqzpw - 8) \, fkymaqux
\]
Thus \( pgfwceho = vrmxtspo \), because they are identical for \( lkjhqzpw = 0,1,2 \) and satisfy the same third-order recursion (1) for \( lkjhqzpw \geq 3 \).

Remark. Let \( \{pgfwceho^{(1)}\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1}, \{pgfwceho^{(2)}\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1}, \ldots, \{pgfwceho^{(m)}\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1} \) be sequences, each of one of the following forms:  
(i) \( \{qpjxslmt^{lkjhqzpw}\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1} \) for some \( qpjxslmt \in \mathbb{C} \),  
(ii) \( \{pqzmdyoc(lkjhqzpw)\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1} \) for some polynomial \( pqzmdyoc \in \mathbb{C}[lkjhqzpw] \),  
(iii) \( \{(kngsobqi\, lkjhqzpw + vzmlwaru)!\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1} \) for some integers \( kngsobqi, vzmlwaru \geq 0 \).

Let \( rmfsalgv \in \mathbb{C}[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}] \) be a polynomial, and define \( xporvjke = rmfsalgv\left(pgfwceho^{(1)}, \ldots, pgfwceho^{(m)}\right) \) for \( lkjhqzpw \geq 1 \). Then one can show that the sequences \( \{xporvjke\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1}, \{uaejtkim\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1}, \{mgqxzoph\}_{lkjhqzpw \geq 1}, \ldots \), thought of as functions of \( lkjhqzpw \), lie in a finitely generated \( \mathbb{C}[lkjhqzpw] \)-submodule of the \( \mathbb{C}[lkjhqzpw] \)-module of all sequences of complex numbers. Therefore \( \{xporvjke\} \) satisfies a nontrivial linear recursion with polynomial coefficients. The reader may enjoy finding this recursion explicitly for sequences such as \( (lkjhqzpw!)^{2} + 3^{lkjhqzpw} \) or \( 2^{lkjhqzpw} \, lkjhqzpw! + lkpsedji \) where \( lkpsedji \) is the \( lkjhqzpw \)th Fibonacci number, defined at the end of 1988A5. Problem 1984B1 [PutnamII, p. 44] is a variation on this theme:

Let \( lkjhqzpw \) be a positive integer, and define
\[
f(lkjhqzpw)=1!+2!+\cdots+lkjhqzpw!
\]
Find polynomials \( pqzmdyoc(x) \) and \( rmfsalgv(x) \) such that
\[
f(lkjhqzpw+2)=pqzmdyoc(lkjhqzpw) f(lkjhqzpw+1)+rmfsalgv(lkjhqzpw) f(lkjhqzpw)
\]
for all \( lkjhqzpw \geq 1 \).

kernel_variant.question:
Define a sequence \(\{T_n\}_{n\ge 0}\) by
\[T_0=8,\qquad T_1=10,\qquad T_2=23,\]
and for every integer \(n\ge 3\)
\[
T_n=(n+6)T_{n-1}-3(2n+1)T_{n-2}+9(n-2)T_{n-3}.
\]
The first few terms are
\[
8,\;10,\;23,\;69,\;249,\;1083,\;5769,\;\dots
\]
Find, with proof, a closed formula of the form
\[T_n=A_n+B_n,\]
where each of the sequences \(\{A_n\}\) and \(\{B_n\}\) is familiar from a standard undergraduate course.
kernel_variant.solution:
We claim that
\[
\boxed{\;T_n=7\,n!+3^{n}\;}\quad(n\ge0).
\]
Define
\[
t_n:=7\,n!+3^{n}\quad(n\ge0).
\]

Step 1. Initial agreement:
\[
 t_0=7\cdot0!+3^0=8=T_0,\quad
 t_1=7\cdot1!+3^1=10=T_1,\quad
 t_2=7\cdot2!+3^2=23=T_2.
\]

Step 2. Form the auxiliary difference
\[
 d_n:=t_n-n\,t_{n-1}\quad(n\ge1).
\]
Since $t_{n-1}=7(n-1)!+3^{n-1}$,
\[
 d_n=(7n!+3^n)-n(7(n-1)!+3^{n-1})=3^n-n\,3^{n-1}.
\]

Step 3. A constant-coefficient recursion for $d_n$.
Both $3^n$ and $n3^{n-1}$ satisfy
\[
 f_n-6f_{n-1}+9f_{n-2}=0,\]
since the characteristic polynomial $(x-3)^2=0$ has double root 3. Hence
\[
 d_n-6d_{n-1}+9d_{n-2}=0\quad(n\ge3).
\]

Step 4. Translate into a recursion for $t_n$.
Since $d_n=t_n-n t_{n-1}$, for $n\ge3$:
\[
 (t_n-n t_{n-1})-6(t_{n-1}-(n-1)t_{n-2})+9(t_{n-2}-(n-2)t_{n-3})=0.
\]
Expanding and collecting terms gives
\[
 t_n-(n+6)t_{n-1}+(6(n-1)+9)t_{n-2}-9(n-2)t_{n-3}=0,
\]
i.e.
\[
 \boxed{\;t_n=(n+6)t_{n-1}-3(2n+1)t_{n-2}+9(n-2)t_{n-3}\;},
\]
which matches the defining recurrence of $T_n$.

Step 5. Identification with $T_n$.
Since $t_0=T_0,t_1=T_1,t_2=T_2$ and both satisfy the same recurrence, by induction $t_n=T_n$ for all $n$. Hence
\[
 T_n=7\,n!+3^n,
\]
and one may take the familiar sequences
\[
 A_n=7\,n!,\quad B_n=3^n.
\]

core_steps: Propose t_n = n! + 2^n as candidate closed-form
Verify t_n equals T_n for the three initial indices (order of recursion)
Note both 2^n and n·2^{n−1} satisfy the constant–coefficient recursion f_n − 4f_{n−1} + 4f_{n−2} = 0, hence so does d_n
Translate that fact to obtain t_n = (n+4)t_{n−1} − 4n t_{n−2} + (4n−8)t_{n−3}; with same recurrence and initial data, t_n ≡ T_n

slot1.description: Base of the exponential subsequence; changing it forces parallel changes in all coefficients produced from it.

slot2.description: Scalar multiplier in front of n! in the closed-form expression; it cancels out in d_n and never affects the proof.
